title: The Little Prince
author: After Saint-Exupéry
paragraphs: Once, when I was six years old, I drew a picture of a boa constrictor that had swallowed an elephant whole. It looked like a hat. I showed it to the grown-ups and asked if it frightened them. They said, why would a hat be frightening? So I drew the inside too, the elephant curled up snugly within the snake. But the grown-ups told me to put away my drawings and study geography instead. That is how I lost my first career as an artist. Grown-ups never understand anything by themselves. It is very tiring to always be explaining things to them.

So I grew up and learned to fly airplanes. I flew over deserts and mountains, and I kept my two drawings tucked away in my heart. Then one day, my engine broke down over the Sahara, a thousand miles from anyone. I had barely enough water for a week. That first night I fell asleep on the sand, more alone than a sailor on a raft. Imagine my surprise when, at sunrise, a funny little voice woke me. It said, please, draw me a sheep. I opened my eyes and saw the most extraordinary little fellow, gazing at me quite seriously.

I stared at this strange small person in the middle of the desert. He did not look lost, or hungry, or afraid. He simply asked again, if you please, draw me a sheep. I had never drawn a sheep, so I drew what I knew, my picture of a boa constrictor from the outside. He shook his head. I do not want an elephant inside a snake, he said. I need a sheep. I was astonished. No grown-up had ever understood that drawing before. So I tried drawing sheep, but none were right. Finally, exasperated, I drew a box with holes in it. The sheep is inside, I said. His face lit up. That is exactly what I wanted.

Little by little, I learned about where he came from. The little prince lived on a tiny planet, hardly bigger than a house. I believe it was Asteroid B-612. On his planet there were good seeds and bad seeds. The bad seeds were baobabs, terrible trees that could split a small world apart if you let them grow. Every morning you had to pull up the baobab sprouts while they were still small. It is a matter of discipline, the little prince told me. When you have finished washing in the morning, you must tend your planet carefully. It was a simple, quiet rule he lived by.

The little prince told me about his sunsets. On his tiny planet, he only had to move his chair a few steps to watch the sun go down again and again. One day he had watched forty-four sunsets. You know, he said softly, when you are very sad, sunsets are wonderful. I asked if he had been so very sad that day of forty-four sunsets. But the little prince did not answer. He just looked away toward the horizon, and I understood that some sorrows are too tender to speak of, especially at twilight, when the world grows soft and still.

Then I learned about the flower. One day, a seed had drifted onto his planet from who knows where, and a rose had bloomed. She was magnificent, with her velvet petals and her sweet perfume. But she was also rather vain. She would cough delicately and ask to be shielded from drafts. She claimed she had thorns to protect her from tigers, even though there were no tigers on such a small planet. The little prince had loved her with all his heart, but he was too young to know how to love her well. Her little games confused him.

He should have judged her by her actions, not her words, the little prince told me with a sigh. She perfumed his planet and brightened his days, but he had focused on her thorny pride. He was too young then to understand that beneath her vanity she was tender and fragile. Still, the flower's contradictions troubled his peaceful heart. One morning, he decided he must go. He tidied his planet, swept his volcanoes, watered his rose one last time, and set a glass dome over her to keep her safe from wind. When he said goodbye, the flower tried not to cry. She told him she had been foolish. Go and be happy, she whispered.

The little prince traveled by catching a flock of wild birds. His first stop was a small asteroid where a king sat upon a velvet throne. The king believed he ruled everything, even the stars. He would command the sun to set, but only at the proper time, so his orders were always obeyed. He offered to make the little prince a minister of justice. But there is no one here to judge, said the little prince. Then you may judge yourself, said the king. That is the hardest thing of all. The little prince thought this king was not unreasonable, but the planet was very small and very lonely, so he moved on.

On the second planet lived a vain man who wore a splendid hat. He wanted only one thing, to be admired. Clap your hands, he said. The little prince clapped, and the vain man raised his hat in greeting. He did this again and again. After a few minutes it became tiresome. What good does admiration do you, asked the little prince. The vain man could not answer that. Grown-ups are certainly very odd, the little prince thought as he continued on his way through the darkness between the stars, leaving the vain man alone with his hat and his echo.

The third planet belonged to a drunkard. He sat surrounded by empty bottles and full ones. The little prince asked, what are you doing? Drinking, said the drunkard miserably. Why do you drink? To forget. To forget what? To forget that I am ashamed. Ashamed of what? Ashamed of drinking. The little prince left, feeling very puzzled. He walked away beneath the cold stars thinking how strange grown-ups truly were, going round and round in circles of their own making, never finding a way out. It made him a little sad, but he kept walking, because there were more worlds to see.

On the fourth planet was a businessman, very busy counting stars. He had been counting for years and did not like to be interrupted. He believed that because he was the first to think of owning the stars, they were his. He counted them and wrote the number on a slip of paper and locked it in a drawer. But what do you do with the stars, asked the little prince. I own them, said the businessman. But that is no use to the stars, said the little prince. He thought of his own flower and his volcanoes. He was useful to them, and they were useful to him. That was what mattered.

The fifth planet was the smallest of all. There was just enough room for a streetlamp and a lamplighter. The lamplighter would light the lamp when evening came and put it out when morning arrived. But his planet turned faster and faster, so now a day lasted only one minute. He lit the lamp and put it out, lit it and put it out, with no rest at all. The little prince admired him. This man, he thought, is the only one who is not ridiculous, because he thinks of something besides himself. He is the only one I could have made my friend. But the planet was too small for two.

The sixth planet was much larger, and an old gentleman lived there who wrote enormous books. He was a geographer. He asked the little prince to describe his planet. The little prince spoke of his flower. We do not record flowers, said the geographer. They are ephemeral. What does ephemeral mean, asked the little prince. It means they are at risk of vanishing before long. My flower is ephemeral, the little prince repeated softly, and he felt a pang in his heart. She has only four thorns to protect her against the world. The geographer suggested he visit Earth next. And so he did.

Earth is not just any planet. It has one hundred and eleven kings, seven thousand geographers, nine hundred thousand businessmen, millions of drunkards, and three hundred million vain people. But from high above, the little prince saw only sand. He landed in the Sahara Desert and met no one. Then a small golden snake appeared, coiled like a bracelet on the sand. The snake spoke in riddles. I can send you back to your own star, said the snake, if ever you grow too homesick. The little prince gazed at the snake with his gentle eyes. You are a strange creature, he said, thin as a finger. But you are powerful, I think.

The little prince walked through the desert. He climbed a high mountain, but from the top he could see only rocky peaks stretching away forever. This planet is all dry and sharp, he said. His voice echoed back to him from the cliffs, dry and sharp, dry and sharp. On his own planet, his flower would speak to him, and his volcanoes gave warmth. Here, the echo only repeated his loneliness back at him. He thought how hard it is to be alone in a big, empty world. He missed his rose, though he would not quite admit it yet, even to himself.

Then the little prince came upon a garden full of roses. There were five thousand of them, all blooming at once, and they all looked exactly like his flower. He felt very unhappy. His rose had told him she was the only one of her kind in the whole universe. And here were five thousand in a single garden. He lay down in the grass and wept. I thought I was rich because I had a unique flower, he said, but all I have is an ordinary rose. He felt very small and very sad, and he cried for a long time among the rustling petals.

It was then that the fox appeared. Good morning, said the little prince. Good morning, said the fox. Come play with me, said the little prince. I am so unhappy. I cannot play with you, said the fox. I am not tamed. What does that mean, to tame? It means to establish ties, said the fox. Right now you are just a little boy like a hundred thousand other little boys. And I am just a fox like a hundred thousand other foxes. But if you tame me, we shall need each other. You will be unique in all the world to me, and I shall be unique to you.

The little prince understood. My rose has tamed me, he said softly. The fox nodded. To tame takes patience, he explained. Each day you must come a little closer. Sit in the grass a little nearer. Come at the same hour, so my heart can begin to prepare for happiness. And so the little prince came every day, and they grew closer, and the fox was happy. When it was time to leave, the fox said, I shall cry. Then it has done you no good, said the little prince. It has, said the fox, because now the color of wheat will remind me of your golden hair forever.

Before they parted, the fox shared his secret. It is very simple, he said. One sees clearly only with the heart. What is essential is invisible to the eye. The little prince repeated it so he would remember. What is essential is invisible to the eye. Your rose is special not because of how she looks but because of the time you gave her, said the fox. You are responsible, forever, for what you have tamed. You are responsible for your rose. The little prince whispered the words again, letting them settle deep inside him like seeds. I am responsible for my rose.

The little prince went back to the garden of five thousand roses. You are not at all like my rose, he told them gently. No one has tamed you and you have tamed no one. You are beautiful, but you are empty. One could not give one's life for you. My rose, all on her own, is more important than all of you together, because she is the one I watered. Because she is the one I sheltered behind a screen. Because she is the one whose caterpillars I killed. Because she is the one I listened to, when she grumbled, or boasted, or even said nothing at all. Because she is my rose.

Then the little prince met a railway switchman who watched the express trains thunder past, carrying passengers this way and that. Where are they all going, asked the little prince. Even the driver of the engine does not know, said the switchman. They are never happy where they are, said the little prince. Only children press their faces against the windows, he observed, because children know what they are looking for. They give their time to a rag doll, and the doll becomes very important. The switchman nodded. Children are lucky, he agreed. And the little prince walked on, thinking about his rose.

Next he met a merchant who sold thirst-quenching pills. You swallow one and you need not drink for a whole week. Why do you sell these, asked the little prince. Because they save time, said the merchant. Fifty-three minutes a week. And what does one do with those fifty-three extra minutes? Whatever one likes, said the merchant. If I had fifty-three extra minutes, said the little prince, I would walk very slowly toward a spring of cool, fresh water. He thought about how grown-ups were always hurrying, always saving time, but never knowing what to save it for. A glass of water, drunk slowly, was worth more than all their rushing.

By now I had been in the desert for eight days, and I had drunk my last drop of water. I told the little prince I was thirsty too. He said, a well is a fine thing in the desert. We walked together under the stars. The sand glowed silver in the moonlight. I carried the little prince when he grew sleepy. What makes the desert beautiful, he murmured, is that somewhere it hides a well. I looked at his sleeping face, so fragile and pale, and I thought, what I see here is only a shell. What is most important is invisible. His half-open lips smiled faintly in the moonlight.

At dawn we found the well. I drew up the bucket and held it to the little prince's lips. The water was sweet, born from our walk under the stars, from the song of the pulley, from the effort of my arms. It was good for the heart, like a present. On his planet, the little prince said, people grow five thousand roses in one garden and do not find what they are looking for. Yet what they seek could be found in a single rose or a sip of water. He was right. The eyes are blind. One must search with the heart.

The little prince grew quiet. He told me he had been on Earth for almost a year, and the anniversary of his arrival was near. He had landed close to this very spot. I felt a strange sadness. That is why you were walking alone in the desert that morning, I said. He blushed faintly and did not answer. I understood then that he was planning something. Promise me, he said, that you will draw me a muzzle for the sheep, so it cannot eat my rose. I drew the muzzle, but my heart was heavy. There were things the little prince had not told me.

On the last evening, the little prince walked out into the desert. I followed him. He spoke gently to me. Tonight it will be one year, he said. My star will be right above the place where I landed. He paused. Do not come tonight. It will look as if I am suffering. It will look as if I am dying. But it is not true. It is too far. I could not carry this body with me. It is too heavy. He saw my tears and said, you will have the stars. Because I live on one of them, whenever you look up at night, all the stars will seem to laugh for you. You alone will have stars that can laugh.

When you are comforted, he said, and you always do find comfort in time, you will be glad you knew me. You will always be my friend. You will want to laugh with me. Open your window sometimes, just for the pleasure of it, and your friends will be amazed to see you laughing at the sky. You will tell them, yes, the stars always make me laugh. They will think you are crazy, and I shall have played a fine trick on you. Instead of stars I shall have given you a whole sky of tiny laughing bells. And he laughed his bright little laugh one more time.

That night I did not see him leave. He slipped away without a sound. When I found the place in the sand at dawn, there was nothing there. His body was not heavy enough to remain. And now, many years have passed. I know he returned to his planet because at sunrise his body had vanished. At night I love to listen to the stars. They sound like five hundred million little bells. But sometimes I wonder, did the sheep eat the rose? One small thing can change everything in the universe. Look up at the sky. Ask yourself, has the flower been eaten or not? And you will see how everything changes.

And so, dear listener, if ever you find yourself in the Sahara and a small person appears before you, with golden hair and a gentle laugh, who refuses to answer questions, you will know who he is. Be kind to him. Send word to me that he has returned. But for now, let this story rest like starlight on your pillow. Close your eyes. Somewhere above you, a little prince tends his rose and watches his three small volcanoes. The stars are laughing softly, like bells, just for you. Sleep now. What is essential is invisible to the eye, but the heart knows it is there, always. Goodnight.
